data.title: First Solar Stock: Deep Analysis Reveals Solar's Geopolitical Importance
data.description:
First Solar, Inc. (NASDAQ:FSLR) stock is in a persistent downtrend with a -22% loss since my last analysis amid fears that the Trump administration will withdraw the company's federal tax credit benefits in the near future. However, such an action without alternative strong support for solar power would be a short-term gain for the United States Treasury, but a long-term loss for the country's energy independence and security. Utility-scale solar power infrastructure remains the most critical long-term energy asset, and China currently retains a wide market lead in this regard. Catching up to China here may be impossible, but it is important that the United States prepares well in advance to protect from dependency dynamics evolving over many decades.

Due to the foresight of the current Trump administration and surrounding advisors, including the Department of Government Efficiency, I expect First Solar and its proponents will continue to be fortified politically. As such, a sentiment reversal seems likely for First Solar stock in the near-term future when Trump and his administration have more publicly outlined their stance on renewable energy infrastructure development in the United States moving forward.

Tax Credit Analysis

The new Trump administration's most negative impact on the solar industry so far has been on short-term investor sentiment. If the Trump administration intends to repeal the Inflation Reduction Act (“IRA”), which provides substantial tax incentives for clean energy, First Solar investors have a reason to be concerned in the short term. Even in the immediate aftermath of Trump being elected, First Solar stock dropped around 12% in price, primarily reflecting this possible elimination of federal tax credits.

Trump is not a particularly good prospect for First Solar at face value, considering that in 2024 the company anticipated $1 billion to $1.05 billion in cash proceeds from the Advanced Manufacturing Production Credit under Section 45X of the Internal Revenue Code; results are expected to be reported on February 25th.

However, this elimination of federal tax credits for First Solar is currently speculative. New executive orders from the President do not impact current energy tax incentives.

Even in a worst-case scenario where the federal tax credits are annihilated with guidance from the Department of Government Efficiency (an action I would deem wise to avoid if possible), First Solar would remain in a robust domestic position if Trump's tariffs heavily penalize anti-dumping practices from China and other international manufacturing centers of excellence.

However, if the Trump administration repeals federal tax credits and subsequently fails to put into place stringent anti-dumping measures (for example, to prevent JinkoSolar (JKS) from China undercutting First Solar in the domestic U.S. market) on top of moderate trade tariffs, the United States solar energy position (an extremely valuable long-term geopolitical play) could be severely weakened.

First Solar remains the United States' most dominant solar panel producer, operating with a full-stack, vertically integrated and domestic manufacturing model. The Trump administration and future U.S. governments would be wise to safeguard it, and given the company's impact on America's domestic manufacturing strength, I believe Trump has the foresight to work to preserve this. This creates a bullish framework for First Solar investors to consider, despite present bearishness indicated in the enduring stock price downtrend.

First Solar's domestic nameplate annual manufacturing capacity is set to increase to 14 GW by 2026. First Solar is the West's largest fully vertically integrated solar manufacturer. The company does not manufacture in China. — Rodzianko's own words, sourced from Business Wire, First Solar.

Energy Analysis

In 2023, the U.S. consumed nearly 19 million barrels per day of oil, but the Energy Information Administration forecasts that U.S. oil demand will rise to 20.5 million barrels per day in 2025. Under President Trump's second term, U.S. oil production is projected to reach record levels; U.S. crude oil output will likely increase to 13.6 million barrels per day in 2026. However, the Energy Information Administration forecasts that the global supply of crude oil and other liquid hydrocarbons will only be adequate to meet world demand through 2050.

Trump, renowned as a tactician, is utilizing America's crude oil production capacity for short-term strategic gain, but the long-term outlook ought not to be forgotten. Without a robust clean energy infrastructure, the United States will likely lose the long-term energy competition against China, the world's foremost producer of solar panels.

Renewable energy sources are forecasted to become 65% to 80% of global power generation by 2050, while fossil fuels' contribution to the global supply is expected to decrease from 80% today to around 20% by 2050. However, the IEA's 20% 2050 target is based on stringent net-zero goals, which appear idealistic and unreasonable.

As of data from 2023, China generates around 44% of its electricity from renewable sources, a vast lead to just 21% in the United States. Further delays in domestic renewable energy adoption in America may increase its reliance on China for critical future energy components like solar panels and batteries. Such reliance may result in significant economic and national security risks. Therefore, First Solar and other U.S. solar panel manufacturers are unlikely to be neglected by the current U.S. Government and following administrations.

A failure to commit to renewable energy infrastructure now may also destabilize the United States' global economic leadership position in the long term. When solar power (most certainly) becomes the predominant form of power provision on the planet, China could be deemed the dominant energy market leader, with an impenetrable moat developed patiently and devoutly over decades. As such, First Solar remains critical to America's long-term geopolitical interests, an assessment the Trump administration will likely also realize. This is fundamental to the long-term bull case for First Solar, but I consider it prudent to monitor the stock and wait for a sentiment reversal before heavily allocating capital.

Valuation Analysis

The following price targets show the theoretical impacts of the IRA being repealed (bear case), the IRA modified to moderately lower tax credits (base case), and being sustained as currently instated (bull case). My targets also consider weak solar panel anti-dumping policies from the Trump administration (bear case), moderately strong anti-dumping policies (base case), and strong anti-dumping policies (bull case). The current stock price is $163.

Sentiment Analysis

First Solar stock is in bearish territory, made evident by the 50-day moving average crossing down past the 200-day moving average, and the Relative Strength Index value being around 40. I do not expect this bear market to change in the immediate future. The new political dynamics under Trump deserve caution, even though they primarily affect investor sentiment for solar companies more than their financials for now.

The primary risk that investors face is that the repeal of IRA tax credits will cause further selling action. At this point, significantly lower valuations may occur in the short term. These lower valuations would potentially open up an opportunity to buy First Solar stock as a medium- to long-term holding, but now remains a highly precarious entry point. The reduction in short-term profitability from the repeal of IRA tax credits wouldn't destroy First Solar's competitive product advantages and long-term growth potential, but it will delay this growth significantly. Though the current valuation is appealing, for the highest alpha, it's best to have some patience and wait for a sentiment reversal and a clearer stance on clean energy policies from the Trump administration as they impact First Solar.

Q4 Preview

For Q4 2024, due on February 25th, the company is likely to have delivered robust earnings growth of around 50% year-over-year. Revenue is also likely to be up nearly 30% year-over-year. These factors are due to strong current market demand, record production, and competent cost management. This could very well act as a moment of sentiment stabilization, as continued earnings growth and margin expansion is probable throughout FY25. The company's new Alabama facility, capable of 3.5 GW of annual production, came online in September 2024. Its Louisiana facility, capable of the same annual production capacity, is anticipated to come online in H2 2025. Moreover, tariffs instigated by Trump on China will affect solar panels, supporting domestic demand for First Solar's products. First Solar has around 95% of its revenue from the United States.

Conclusion: Strong Buy

My base-case price target indicates a two-year annual return of around 50%, making the investment a Strong Buy as it stands. However, for capital allocators like myself who want the ideal moment to invest, negative returns will likely continue to persist for a while longer, making it prudent to wait. We need a stronger stance from the Trump administration on whether, and how, it plans to support First Solar and whether the IRA will continue to be a part of this for the indefinite future.

With a reasonable modification and not a complete repeal of tax credits, First Solar will still have a very strong near-term return horizon. Demand for solar panels remains robust, and America needs to secure its long-term energy independence with renewable energy just as much as it requires short-term tactical geopolitical security through fossil fuels.

data.title: Entrada Therapeutics: Cash-Rich Innovator With Asymmetric Upside
data.description:
Biotech investors sometimes overlook companies solving fundamental drug development problems. Entrada Therapeutics (NASDAQ:TRDA) operates in one such space, solving the enduring problem of intracellular drug delivery. Although much of the sector is under capital constraints and shifting regulatory pressures, Entrada's solid position does not seem to be appreciated by the market yet. A specialized approach and a strategy that combines internal development with strategic partnerships position the company for a risk-reward profile that the market has yet to price in.

Financials

Entrada Therapeutics has a financial position that affords it a level of stability that few small-cap biotech firms can claim - especially pre-commercial ones. It had $449.3 million in cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities as of Q3 2024, up from $352 million as of 2023. Those gains were driven mainly by a $100 million direct offering and a $75 million milestone payment related to its partnership with Vertex Pharmaceuticals. While many early-stage biotech firms can barely extend their runway without excessive dilution, Entrada has bolstered its balance sheet through the aforementioned direct offering and strategic collaborations such as the Vertex partnership - a strategy that has minimized immediate reliance on additional fundraising.

Entrada has strong cash reserves but reported a $14 million net loss for Q3 2024. Increased investment in the lead Duchenne muscular dystrophy candidate ENTR-601-44 and pipeline expansion have pushed research and development expenses to $31.3 million compared with the prior year. High personnel costs also drove higher general and administrative costs, suggesting the company is preparing for increased clinical activity. The company's debt is also manageable, standing at $61 million - this is reasonable given its pre-commercial state, and the burn is being managed carefully. However, the only reason the Q3 2024 and Q1 2024 had net profits was due to one-time payments I mentioned earlier. Aside from this, the company's net loss has been trending down. Based on this trend, I would conservatively estimate Entrada's cash burn rate looking forward at $40-50 million per quarter, so its cash runway is likely to stretch into 2027. This longer time frame reduces near-term financing risk and allows the company to focus on execution rather than capital markets volatility. Of course, this is a conservative estimate - if R&D costs plateau, it is possible the burn will be much less than that.

Perhaps most interesting is Entrada's sharp drop in collaboration revenue, from $43.7 million in Q3 2023 to $19.6 million in Q3 2024. It owes its drop to lower expenses associated with its Vertex-partnered VX-670 program, which suggests that while past milestones have been lucrative, future revenue contributions from partnerships could be sporadic. This puts more pressure on the internal pipeline progress - ENTR-601-44 - to build investor confidence.

A potential concern with Entrada is its relatively low liquidity. The dangers of dilution are always a reasonable apprehension that investors have, but as I stated earlier, I believe that the company has sufficient cash reserves to fund development without relying on immediate capital infusion. Due to this, I think the risk in the near to mid-term stemming from liquidity or related issues is low.

Drug Pipeline

Entrada Therapeutics' pipeline is built around its Endosomal Escape Vehicle platform for improved intracellular delivery of oligonucleotide-based therapies. This platform is the core mechanism for ENTR-601-44, the lead candidate for Duchenne muscular dystrophy in patients with exon-44 mutations. The company recently received clearance from the UK MHRA to commence ELEVATE-44-201, a Phase 1/2 multiple ascending dose trial - its first major clinical step in this space. While exon-skipping therapies have had mixed results due to delivery challenges and variable dystrophin restoration, Entrada's strategy seeks to overcome these limitations through increased cellular uptake. If its tech supports meaningful dystrophin production while maintaining an excellent safety profile, the drug could carve out a niche where options are slim.

Beyond DMD, Entrada's work on VX-670 with Vertex Pharmaceuticals is another interesting avenue. It targets RNA mis-splicing disorders, specifically overcoming challenges of injecting oligonucleotides into muscle tissue. The involvement of Vertex suggests confidence in Entrada's delivery mechanism, a bottleneck in many genetic therapies. But given declining collaboration revenue in recent quarters, it's unclear how aggressively Vertex plans to push VX-670 forward. If the program enters later-stage trials, it may provide non-dilutive capital in the form of future milestone payments that would help Entrada grow its cash runway even more.

The wider pipeline includes additional oligonucleotide-based programs ENTR-601-45 and ENTR-601-50, likely targeting other DMD subpopulations. These early-stage assets also signal that Entrada is thinking beyond the single-drug model that could provide long-term value if its EEV platform proves broadly applicable. The key risk is whether the technology can translate into clinical success at a rate that distinguishes it from existing exon-skipping approaches. With first patient trials expected to begin in 2025, investors should be getting an early readout on how Entrada's science holds up in human trials.

EEV and Splicing Mechanisms

Drug delivery may be the most important driver of treatment success for Entrada. Some genetic diseases like Duchenne muscular dystrophy and RNA mis-splicing disorders require precise intracellular delivery of therapeutic molecules. Traditional approaches based on antisense oligonucleotides or phosphorodiamidate morpholino oligomers have shown poor uptake into muscle cells and weak clinical efficacy. The company's endosomal escape vehicle technology is designed to address this issue by improving endosomal release - one of the biggest barriers to oligonucleotide therapeutics. If delivered systemically, such molecules are usually trapped in endosomes and degraded before reaching their target inside the cell. Entrada's EEVs contain an endosomal escape-enhancing cyclic peptide motif that allows more active drugs to reach the nucleus and engage their genetic targets. This is important in muscle tissue where drug penetration has traditionally been a problem.

Mutations in DMD prevent the production of dystrophin, a structural protein needed for muscle integrity in Duchenne muscular dystrophy. Without it, muscle fibers become weak, lose function and degenerate. Exon-skipping therapies like Entrada's ENTR-601-44 let cells "skip over" the mutated exon and resume production of a shorter but functional dystrophin protein. Unlike typical exon-skipping drugs that show poor intracellular uptake, Entrada's EEV-PMO conjugate enhances delivery into muscle cells and increases dystrophin restoration. It is possible that this may be a key differentiator, as exon-skipping therapies have been described as having poor clinical efficacy despite measurable dystrophin uptake.

The Vertex-partnered VX-670 program targets RNA mis-splicing-related diseases, specifically CUG repeat expansion disorders such as myotonic dystrophy type 1. In DM1, toxic RNA traps MBNL1, a key splicing regulator, causing widespread splicing defects in several genes. These toxic RNA repeats bind to the specifically designed VX-670 to release MBNL1 and resume normal splicing. Muscle penetration for oligonucleotides in DM1 has always been a problem. The EEV platform may offer an advantage in getting higher drug concentrations into the skeletal and cardiac muscle where the disease is most damaging. If it is successful, it would open up doors to similar RNA-targeting approaches in other neuromuscular diseases.

Regulatory Green Light Marks a Crucial Inflection Point

Entrada Therapeutics received clearance from the UK Medicines and Healthcare Products Regulatory Agency to initiate the ELEVATE-44-201 clinical trial of its lead candidate, ENTR-601-44. This is its first major step into patient trials and a critical test of its Endosomal Escape Vehicle technology. Although encouraging preclinical and early human safety data have shown uptake and exon skipping, real-world efficacy in Duchenne muscular dystrophy patients will be the pivotal point. The study will assess safety, pharmacokinetics, and dystrophin restoration in patients with exon 44 mutations, starting in Q2 2025.

The study has a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled design that allows an objective assessment of ENTR-601-44's clinical impact. Unlike other early-stage biotech trials that rely on open-label data (generally more subject to patient bias, since researchers and patients know the treatments being given), Entrada's decision from the start to conduct a controlled study demonstrates faith in its technology. A multiple ascending dose structure will evaluate dosing regimens ranging from 6 mg/kg to 18 mg/kg, with an emphasis on safety and drug uptake. This trial structure will determine the optimal dose and provide early insight into how Entrada's delivery platform translates into meaningful dystrophin production. If the drug demonstrates robust exon skipping at clinically relevant levels, it may differentiate itself from existing PMO-based therapies that have shown low efficacy and inconsistent benefit.

Its global design also positions Entrada for a wider regulatory pathway outside of the UK. It has completed a Phase 1 safety study in healthy volunteers in which ENTR-601-44 was well-tolerated without serious adverse events or safety signals. Though that somewhat validated the drug's pharmacokinetic profile, the upcoming trial will be the first to assess the functional impact in live DMD patients. With few exon 44 mutation treatment options available, even modest dystrophin restoration improvements could be a reason enough to accelerate pathways in the United States and Europe.

MHRA clearance reflects Entrada's rapid clinical progress, but it also sets expectations high. We will need to closely monitor early signals of efficacy, as the absence of meaningful dystrophin restoration would weaken the broader investment thesis surrounding EEV technology. However, a strong result from the trial may not only position ENTR-601-44 as a key player in DMD but also establish the company's platform for future intracellular genetic therapies.

Scientific and Execution Risks

Although Entrada Therapeutics presents a compelling case for its intracellular delivery technology, scientifically and operationally significant risks remain. The company's Endosomal Escape Vehicle platform is based on the premise that its cyclic peptide motif facilitates the escape of oligonucleotides from endosomes into the cytoplasm for further therapeutic action. The problem is that endosomal escape remains a barrier to drug development, and while preclinical data indicates improved uptake, true clinical efficacy in humans is far from certain. Many companies pursued enhanced oligonucleotide delivery strategies, but uptake improvements did not necessarily translate into functional benefits. Even with higher intracellular drug concentrations, Entrada still needs to demonstrate sufficient dystrophin production in Duchenne muscular dystrophy patients to enable real-world strength and mobility gains.

A further scientific risk is the inherent limitation of exon-skipping therapies. Although partial dystrophin production can be restored using this approach, previous attempts with phosphorodiamidate morpholino oligomers have yielded a low quantity and functionally incomplete protein. As an example, Sarepta Therapeutics' (SRPT) approved exon-skipping drugs show some dystrophin restoration but little clinical impact, raising questions about whether these therapies affect disease progression. Entrada's EEV platform improves uptake but does not fundamentally change the biology of exon skipping. If the produced dystrophin is not sufficient to restore muscle integrity, then improved delivery may not be clinically significant.

Beyond the scientific hurdles, Entrada faces execution risks with its trial design and regulatory strategy. With its ELEVATE-44-201 trial, the company is conducting its first real-world EEV technology test in patients - and the data will be scrutinized rigorously with a randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled design. If results show only modest improvements in dystrophin levels or no correlation with functional benefit, the stock could suffer sharp downsides. I would add that although the company has regulatory clearance in the UK, entry into the U.S. and other major markets may pose additional hurdles if early data fails to meet the bar for accelerated approval pathways.

Valuation and Market Analysis

Entrada trades at a 50% discount to the sector median P/B of 2.25, which means investors are paying little premium to its platform technology, intellectual property, and future revenue potential. A P/B ratio near 1 for a pre-commercial biotech means the market values the pipeline just above its tangible assets - as if its potential has almost no intrinsic value. That compares to the wider biotech sector, where such promising early-stage companies generally command premiums based on platform potential only. If Entrada can demonstrate early success with its clinical programs - ENTR-601-44 - the market may need to rethink its platform beyond cash reserves.

The total addressable market for Duchenne muscular dystrophy provides context. New therapeutic entrants and expanded treatment options could drive a global DMD market of $10 billion annually by 2030, according to current estimates. If Entrada takes even a 10% market share in this niche, that translates to over $1 billion in annual revenue - a big sum for a company valued at levels that suggest little faith in commercial success. More broadly, if the EEV platform opens up new oligonucleotide applications beyond DMD, the upside may even reach multi-billion-dollar markets.

Conclusion and Outlook

Entrada Therapeutics now faces execution decisions that will determine whether it remains an undervalued niche player or becomes a recognized leader in intracellular drug delivery. This pre-commercial biotech has an attractive balance sheet that extends its runway into 2027. Such financial power enables it to validate its Endosomal Escape Vehicle platform - an approach to overcoming one of the most persistent barriers in oligonucleotide-based medicine. If it achieves meaningful dystrophin restoration in its ELEVATE-44-201 trial, it would establish ENTR-601-44 as a competitive therapy for Duchenne muscular dystrophy and also confirm the broad applicability of its EEV technology in multiple disease areas.

data.title: Boston Pizza: More Than Ever, Same-Store Sales Matter For Dividend Sustainability
data.description:
Note: All monetary values ​​in this article are represented in US dollars, except where expressly noted in the analysis.

Delving Deeper into Value Promotions: Is it Generating Traffic?

"A quick lunch doesn't have to mean fast food." That's how Boston Pizza (OTC:BPZZF) promoted its value platform during the fourth quarter of last year. Don't look twice! It's a clear attempt to generate incremental sales from guests who are dissatisfied with the value-per-dollar ratio of fast-food restaurants.

I'm not going to lie. Despite being at least half a year behind, Boston Pizza managed to deliver its best quarter of the year. Sure, the results aren't as impressive as when Chili's (EAT) launched its much-talked-about '3-For-Me' with the beloved Big Smasher at the lower end of the price range, but the promotion certainly made the desired difference.

It's a simple, very Applebee-ish (DIN) deal. An entrée that can range from four sandwich options, three lunch-sized pastas, three lunch-sized salads, three small pizza options (not including the pizzas of the day), a side (fries or soup) and a fountain drink. It's not deja vu. I know you've seen other local chains like BJ's Restaurants (BJRI) and their $13 Pizookie Meal Deal with dessert (which should work out to about CAD$18.50) offering desirable lunchtime deals.

At the end of the day, these CAD$15 deals are on par with the $10 deals south of the border, so I like to compare them to Applebee's Really Big Meal Deal. This one does everything Boston Pizza does, but also offers unlimited fries and a chicken sandwich that's guaranteed to be the biggest on the market.

But unlike Applebee's, which claims that its new Big Cluckin' is the biggest fried chicken sandwich on the market and even guarantees an extra chicken breast at no extra cost to guests who bring a larger sandwich, Boston Pizza is all about convenience. If your food doesn't arrive within twenty minutes, your next lunch at BP will be free! It's an interesting risk reversal that helps reduce the guest's perception of risk. After all, this is a precise lunchtime during work hours; if your food takes too long, at least you'll have a free lunch.

It's a classic 'Better Value Than Fast-Food'!

Either way, it’s working for them. There’s no denying that. You can see that by noticing that same-store sales returned to low-single-digit growth during the quarter after some erratic comparisons that ranged from very low-single-digit growth year-over-year to low-single-digit declines year-over-year.

Here’s how it’s been performing over the past few periods, as well as some comparisons with other full-service restaurants:

Note that after the CAD$15 Meal Deal was introduced in November, same-store sales grew 3.4% year-over-year during the fourth quarter. This came after the company reported same-store sales falling 0.6% year-over-year during the third quarter. This means that Boston Pizza has indeed seen a sequential recovery, even though it faces relatively tough comparisons. These comparisons should become easier starting next quarter, as it will be facing historically weak first-quarter comparisons.

Management itself noted that most of the same-store sales growth came from menu price increases. Therefore, we can see that:

  1. The CAD$15 Meal Deal played a key role in increasing the mix which, in conjunction with the price increases, has caused the average check per guest to grow to the point where it offsets the slower traffic; or
  2. The value promotion contained the decline in traffic, which less strongly offset the growth in the average check caused by the increase in menu prices.

Of course, both things may have happened to different degrees. However, it seems like common sense that the price increase was the biggest driver of same-store sales, despite the '$15 Meal Deal' coming into play.

For Q1, in addition to the value platform, Boston Pizza has added Pasta Tuesday. This offering is designed to bring the restaurant some life on typically slower days. For just CAD$11.99 (approximately $8.45), guests can create their own custom pastas. But that’s not all. For CAD$15.99 (approximately $11.25), an upsell lever can be pulled, and more elaborate pastas can be added.

I expect them to continue growing in the low single digits year-over-year for the first quarter of FY 2025 as Boston Pizza rolls out its value promotions and faces easier comparisons.

Despite Higher Sales, Keep an Eye on Growth!

The Boston Pizza Royalty Fund currently has 372 restaurants in its pool. This represents a drop compared to the 377 restaurants at the end of FY 2023. Look how curious! These restaurants generated system-wide sales of CAD$931.7 million during all of FY 2024. Note that this represents a 0.65% year-over-year increase as existing restaurants began to offset the closures of the five underperforming restaurants. AUVs are currently at CAD$2.5 million, up approximately 2% year-over-year from CAD$2.45 million last year.

This represented revenues for the fund of approximately CAD$37.2 million for the full FY 2024, growing approximately 0.81% year over year. Converting this to net income, the fund generated approximately CAD$31.9 million, growing 7.5% year over year. That the most significant growth in net income was due to an increase of at least CAD$4.6 million in the fair value of some assets, which was offset by some increases in interest expense and deferred income taxes. Now, it was non-monetary items that caused net income to increase. We need to look elsewhere!

OCF for FY 2024 was CAD$38.1 million, up about 0.5% year over year. This increase was driven by an increase in royalty and distribution revenue and adjustments to working capital, and offset by administrative expenses. Note that this also does not account for distributable cash.

When we exclude non-cash items such as fair value adjustments from our analysis, as well as consider all cash outflows, we arrive at the fund's distributable cash. Here, despite the fund seeing a 1.35% year-over-year increase during Q4, distributable cash declined from $30.7 million during FY 2023 to CAD$30.4 million during FY 2024. Here we can see that operating gains were offset by a higher cash allocation to Class B unitholders, which alone caused headwinds of CAD$0.6 million.

Even with the reduction in distributable cash, what we saw was an increase in monthly distributions of CAD$0.002. The result was a distribution rate of 99.9%! This, on an annualized basis, represents dividends per unit of CAD$1.38 (CAD$0.115 per month), growing 1.77% compared to the previous period.

Notice that growth was through the roof! Anymore and they would have to supplement the distribution in other ways. This is consistent with our thesis that Boston Pizza is unlikely to sustain low single-digit dividend growth going forward. In fact, it will be in some trouble if it keeps the payout ratio so high and doesn't keep its same-store sales growing. This could stagnate the distribution, which will get worse if the pool isn't increased.

In short, the numbers tell us that:

  1. Boston Pizza showed steady performance, with system-wide sales growing even as the restaurant pool lost five locations year-over-year. This was reflected in higher revenue for the fund;
  2. Although metrics such as net income and OCF are growing year-over-year, cash available for distribution has decreased year-over-year. We can say that although BPRIF is showing consistency, there is not much room for dividend growth, since the payout ratio is very close to 100%; and
  3. To maintain low-single-digit distribution growth, Boston Pizza RIF will need to keep its same-store sales growing. To do so, it’s deepening its value promotions. But beware of red flags! Management says that most of the same-store sales growth is coming from menu price increases. This can cause spillovers in traffic and not sustain enough same-store sales growth.

The Stock Remains Fairly Valued

In my last article on Boston Pizza RIF, I concluded that dividends would almost certainly continue to grow slowly. However, same-store sales are growing in the low single digits and the restaurant pool is only shrinking year over year.

I believe my estimates that dividends will remain flat or grow at up to 1.5% year over year in perpetuity are still valid. This is not only in line with previous analysis, but also with the 10-year growth rate.

Note how much the cash flows to the shareholder are really worth in the form of annual dividends discounted to present value. I will consider the growth rates explained above:

  • Flat: $12.60 (2.86% upside and 2.78% margin of safety);
  • 0.5%: $13.47 (upside of 9.96% and margin of safety of 9.06%);
  • 1%: 14.48% (upside of 18.2% and margin of safety of 15.4%);
  • 1.5%: $15.65 (27.8% upside and 21.7% margin of safety).

Even when compared to other restaurant royalty funds such as Pizza Pizza RIF (PZA:CA), Boston Pizza tends to be trading at a premium.

Now, if we consider Pizza Pizza RIF, keeping its dividend flows constant in perpetuity and discounting to present value, we would still get an upside of 12%! Likewise, when we use the dividend growth rate in line with the 10-year average, we would get an upside of up to 49%.

Much more palatable to me, especially when we are dealing with royalty funds that are naturally comparable to fixed income securities. For funds of this type, if in doubt, it is better to buy at a discount!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Baidu: AI Leadership In Question Amid Macroeconomic And Competitive Pressures
data.description:
Baidu (NASDAQ:BIDU) reported a largely in-line 4Q24 with Baidu Core revenue ahead of consensus by RMB1bn while revenue from the online video site IQIYI missed by RMB 200mn. The shares sold off after the print as management commentary suggested that the company is “navigating near-term pressure” while remaining positive on AI driving long-term growth.

We have been bearish on BIDU since our last publication (see: Baidu: Growth Algorithm Stuck In Neutral With AI Hype And Ad Blues) because we believe that the company is facing a number of challenges across its key business segments amid a challenging macro backdrop.

4Q24 does not change this narrative, given that advertising revenue continues to be pressured by China’s weak macro environment, while BIDU’s leadership in AI is becoming questionable with the emergence of DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) and Apple’s recent partnership with Alibaba.

We believe that BIDU shares could see near-term weakness following the recent rally amid the bullish sentiment on China’s AI play as the market reassesses BIDU’s competitiveness in this increasingly crowded field.

With valuation trading at 8.9x 2026E consensus earnings, we believe the stock is fairly valued given its stagnating growth outlook, and we recommend investors avoid this stock and stick with Chinese internet companies that have stronger domestic competitive positioning and global growth potential, such as PDD (see: PDD: Navigating Tariff Uncertainty, Driving Long-Term Growth Through Global Expansion) and Meituan (see: Meituan: Capturing The Lens Of Opportunity).

4Q24 revenue of RMB 34.12 bn was largely in line with Bloomberg consensus of RMB 33.39bn, with revenue from Baidu Core (RMB 27.7 billion yuan, +0.8% y/y, vs. estimated 26.75 billion yuan) slightly beat while revenue from iQIYI (RMB 6.6 billion yuan vs. estimate 6.84 billion yuan) slightly missed. Adjusted EBITDA of RMB 6.95 billion yuan, -23% y/y, missed estimate of RMB 7.47 billion yuan on higher opex that resulted in a miss in EPS.

More concerning is the operating metrics, in which monthly active users of 679 million missed an estimate of 690.82 million, implying that BIDU may be losing users to rival AI applications such as DeepSeek. The pending partnership between Apple and Alibaba could accelerate user churn, as iPhone users may leverage the built-in Apple/Alibaba AI function instead of BIDU’s Ernie AI.

Since our bearish publication in late November, BIDU’s shares rallied 20% as the market turned bullish on China’s AI plays and potential recovery in the macro environment. However, BIDU’s most recent Q continues to paint a challenging narrative, with no visible turnaround in the weak macro backdrop and the possibility that AI’s investment could continue to weigh on its profitability.

On the advertising side, China’s ongoing macro weakness, characterized by a slow recovery in domestic consumption and cautious business spending, continues to weigh on this segment’s performance.

Ad revenue declined 7% year over year, underscoring the weak demand from SMEs, which account for BIDU’s majority of ad clients and are highly sensitive to China’s current economic backdrop.

While China has implemented a series of monetary easing and fiscal stimulus measures, their effect on the economy has been immaterial as weak business and consumer sentiment continue to weigh on ad spending.

Ad revenue was down 3% for the full year, and we expect the lack of material stimulus to result in another year of low—to mid-single-digit ad revenue decline.

On the AI front, BIDU has historically been considered the leader in China’s AI, but this leadership is being questioned with the emergence of DeepSeek and the recent partnership between Apple and Alibaba. BIDU was widely perceived as the top partner for Apple, given that BIDU has been collaborating with Apple on generative AI services.

After testing the models from Alibaba, BIDU, Tencent and ByteDance, Apple’s decision to work with Alibaba over BIDU raises the question of whether BIDU has the leading position in China’s AI race.

We believe these setbacks exacerbated BIDU’s AI leadership, putting it in a precarious position to react to the competition.

We note that BIDU is expected to continue to invest in AI Cloud as it remains the only growth engine within BIDU, with 26% revenue growth for the Q and 17% growth for the year. In addition, BIDU’s decision to make its ERNIE 4.5 model open-source and make its ERNIE bot free for users may drive broader market adoption. Still, it may also lead to increased operational costs without immediate monetization benefits, in our view. Given that AI-driven search results are not yet fully commercialized, near-term revenue generation remains questionable.

As for autonomous driving, we were encouraged to see Apollo Go saw a 36% growth in ride volume and that BIDU achieved 100% fully driverless operation in China to expand into Hong Kong. However, this remains a very early day in commercialization, and the recently failed partnership with Geely implies that BIDU’s autonomous driving technology is unlikely to see mass commercialization in the foreseeable future.

Baidu is currently trading at 8.8x forward earnings with a low to mid-single digit revenue growth profile that is similar to Weibo, that trades at 6x forward earnings. Given the ad weakness and the lack of growth catalysts in AI coupled with ongoing investment that will weigh on earnings, we believe BIDU should be trading in line with WB as a traditional online advertising company. At 6x 2026E earnings, we value BIDU at $85/share, or roughly 12% downside from the current level.

As we noted earlier, this print will likely result in the sell-side being “cautiously” optimistic on the AI outlook, while the buyside is likely to reassess BIDU’s AI growth narrative. We remain neutral on both AI and ads, as we have been before and believe that investors should avoid this value trap and stick with Chinese internet companies that have global growth potential in PDD and Meituan.

data.title: Pernod Ricard: Don't Expect Valuation To Go Any Higher In The Near-Term
data.description:
Investment Overview

I give a hold rating for Pernod Ricard SA (OTCPK:PRNDY) as the company faces headwinds in its core markets, particularly in the US and China. The business has been losing market share in the US and is dealing with poor consumer sentiment and regulatory challenges in China. Additionally, margin pressures and potential tariff risks further limit any near-term upside. Hence, I think it is difficult to justify a bullish rating at this time.

Business Description

Pernod Ricard competes in the premium spirits and wines industry. The business has a portfolio that includes well-known brands like Absolut Vodka, Jameson Irish Whiskey, Martell Cognac, and Chivas Regal. It reports its business in three regions: Asia & ROW, Americas, and Europe. Amongst these, Asia & ROW represents the largest mix of PRNDY revenue base (43% as of FY24), followed by an even split between the other two regions.

1H25 Earnings Review Update

PRNDY saw its revenue fall by 4% y/y on an organic basis and 6% y/y on a reported basis to EUR 6.18 billion in 1H25. The main drag is the weak performance in the US, where sales fell 7% y/y, and China, which saw a significant 25% y/y decline. India did well growing sales by 6% y/y, but clearly, it wasn’t enough. Profitability also took a hit. In 1H25, profit from recurring operations fell 7% y/y to EUR1.99 billion, driven by revenue decline and gross margin compression (from 61.9% to 61.1%). As a result, adj EPS fell 11% y/y to EUR 5.05.

Faces Share Erosion and Weak Consumer Demand in the US

It is really hard to invest in a business that faces visible headwinds in its core regions. In the case of the US, while the broader industry has shown signs of resilience, growing 1% y/y (per the Jefferies report), PRNDY US sales declined 7% y/y in 1H25, which reflects very badly on PRNDY as it suggests PRNDY is losing a ton of market share. Morgan Stanley’s report basically confirms this fact (refer to the table above), that PRNDY has been losing share. I think there is a real risk that PRNDY will continue to lose share as consumer preferences are shifting towards tequila and ready-to-drink beverages, and PRNDY has little exposure to these verticals (its main business is still premium spirits and wine).

Although management cited improving sell-out trends during the holiday season, I wouldn’t extrapolate that strength into the rest of this year as there is seasonality to consider. It is natural that during the holiday season, consumers drink more (more parties and gathering occasions). It does not change the fact that PRNDY is losing share. Also, the industry is facing a secular headwind—consumers drinking less. For PRNDY to maintain or gain back share, I believe it has no choice but to step up on promotions, which is going to hurt margins.

China Recovery Remains Uncertain Amid Structural Challenges

China has historically been a key growth driver for PRNDY, but recent and 1H25 results suggest that China is likely to become a drag on performance for the foreseeable future. The decline seen in 1H25 was massive—down 25% y/y—and the underlying causes seem more structural than temporal—driven by a combination of weak consumer spending sentiment and regulatory challenges. To give a better sense of how significant this demand deterioration is, consider that Martell Cognac, PRNDY’s flagship brand in China, saw particularly sharp declines (contributing 90% of PRNDY’s overall net sales decline). You may think that this is a one-off drop, but I think this suggests a structural impairment in near-term demand, as management noted early indicators suggest that Chinese New Year demand is expected to be weak. While management has taken steps to combat this volume decline, such as implementing mid-single-digit price increases on Martell (post-Chinese New Year), I don’t think this is sufficient to drive growth. I actually think this will cause volume to fall even further.

Given these challenges, I really think it is not plausible that PRNDY can achieve its mid-term growth target for China. If the demand environment remains sluggish, I see a strong probability that PRNDY needs to revise its expectations downward, which will put further pressure on its stock.

Margin Pressures and Limited Operating Leverage

Despite cost-cutting efforts, PRNDY cannot escape the fate of margin compression as the top line continues to underperform. To give a sense of things, PRNDY has already achieved EUR 900 million in cost savings since FY23. Despite this profit margin from recurring operations fell from 34% in 1H23 to 32.1% in 1H25. Looking ahead, on top of the poor demand outlook, I see the potential for additional margin pressure if the US does implement tariffs on European spirits (management estimated this could result in a EUR 200 million impact in FY26).

Valuation

Simply put, I don’t see a visible path for PRNDY to see a major multiple re-rating upwards in the near term. There are just too many headwinds that could continue to pressure demand and cause margins to fall further. While I can understand why some market participants may want to invest as the stock valuation seems cheap—14x forward earnings, which is way below the historical average of 21x—I think the fundamentals are just not good enough to support a buy rating. I believe the market clearly values the growth outlook of companies in this industry as Diageo PLC trades at 16x with a mid-single-digit outlook. This basically limits how high PRNDY's multiple can go if its growth outlook remains at low-single digit percentages.

Conclusion

I give a hold rating for PRNDY. The headwinds that PRNDY faces in the US and China are concerning, and I don’t see a path for demand to recover anytime soon. Moreover, PRNDY is facing margin contraction despite cost-cutting efforts. The potential tariffs from the US could further pressure margins. Although PRNDY trades at a discount to historical valuations, I believe this reflects the weak growth outlook. Without a clear catalyst for re-acceleration, I see little reason to be optimistic.

data.title: High Yield Is Not As High As You Think. WEA Vs. FALN, CEF Vs. ETF
data.description:
This article will be posted as an example of articles that will go directly to the public. There is no actionable trade idea in this article, and we are just sharing part of our research at Trade With Beta with the general public to gain more followers.

In recent years, we have observed a trend of narrowing spreads between Treasuries and investment-grade bonds. This puts us in a tough spot to earn alpha. To earn some extra yield, we have to go lower on the credit scale. With markets making high after high and overall sentiment being extremely bullish, we're left with very few choices. In today's article, we will hunt for yield in bond portfolios that are slightly below investment grade. The stars of today's article are two funds:

  • iShares Fallen Angels USD Bond ETF (NASDAQ:FALN)
  • Western Asset Premier Bond Fund (NYSE:WEA)

Before observing the fund's data, we want to familiarize the reader with some basic yield data and historical spreads.

Treasury Yield Curve

The curve finally looks somewhat normal after a long period of inverted yields. With the expectation of short-term rates going lower, we may start looking for value in CEFs that employ leverage to benefit from the spread while lending short-term and buying long-term fixed-income instruments.

Investment-Grade Historical Spreads

We used the data from the FRED website for investment-grade bond yields. To calculate the spread, we used the five-year constant maturity Treasury yield:

As seen above, we are very close to the historical lows.

High-Yield Historical Spreads

We did the same thing here, except this time, we calculated the spread for the high-yield bonds:

High-yield bonds have also narrowed their spread from the historical averages, and we are very close to the historical lows.

SPY Earnings Yield Vs. 10-Year Treasury Yield

Bond treasury yields and equity earnings yields are pretty much inverted at the moment. This is based on bullish expectations of the earnings growth ratio for the companies in the index. If we view the earnings and disregard their growth potential, we can sarcastically conclude that equity investors are not demanding any premium for taking the higher risk of equities and are even demanding a credit spread from government bonds:

Yield Curve

On the yield curve, one can see the credit spreads for investment-grade and high-yield bonds compared to their historical averages for the last 10 years. For SPY, we have calculated the expected return going forward based on two scenarios:

  • P/E ratio of 25 in the future and EPS growth of 12%
  • P/E ratio of 20 and EPS growth of 9%.

If we use just simple spread metrics, we can strongly conclude that the only instrument one should buy is Treasuries and wait for better pricing, and this does make some sense. Anyway, we find the sentiment to be so bullish that we can take some further risk while this lasts. We're not interested in common stock and prefer to evaluate what we can earn in bonds.

ETF Overview

FALN has around $2B in net assets and 163 holdings. The average yield to maturity is 6.88%, and the weighted average maturity is 6.81 years. The effective duration is 4.60 years. The expense ratio of FALN is 0.25%. We can see in the chart above that the fund's average weighted credit score is around "BB-." With this fallen angel, we can expect to lock something close to 6.5% (considering the expense ratio). The limited duration of the fund makes our expected standard deviation lower, and even if it's at a historically low spread, we find it to be way less risky compared to common stock valuations. Even at this credit spread, we will slightly underperform a standard scenario for SPY while taking a far lower risk:

Let's move to our CEF alternative.

CEF Overview

Our CEF today is Western Asset Premier Bond (WEA). We will begin with the fact that their credit scores are quite similar. WEA has a slightly higher average weighted credit score close to "BB+." WEA is a leveraged fund that can bring some benefit, especially if the SOFR chart below turns out to be wrong and short-term rates fall further than expected.

We will mainly compare WEA and FALN's assets and management fees. Leverage can be easily added to FALN in a simple IBKR account. WEA has around $199 mln in total assets and $133 mln in net assets, distributed in 389 holdings:

We computed that the fund's non-leverage expenses are around 0.64%. The numbers regarding yield to worst of the CEF portfolio are hard to find. Since the information is different on different sites, it's hard to come up with a certain number. The company posts something as an effective yield of 7.27%:

Total investment income / total assets gives a number close to 6.8%. Assuming the bonds trade at a discount, we can also assume the YTM is around 7.2%. It will be pretty normal to see a better explanation on the CEF's website. The average years until maturity are around 8.5, and the average coupon is 6.64%. This is what the price/yield chart and duration vs. price of WEA's average weighted holding looks like:

The fund's effective duration is 5.35 years (quite similar to the FALN duration).

Suppose we assume a YTM for the average WEA asset of 7.27% and a non-leverage expense ratio of 0.64% without considering the fund's leverage. In that case, we can see that even without adjusting for the discount to NAV, WEA can bring similar returns to FALN while even, at least on paper, its holdings are higher rated. Add to this a fair value discount of 4%, and we can see that WEA has some alpha.

Placing Our Funds On The Yield Curve Based On Their Expected Return

FALN and WEA have an expected return that's very similar to each other. If we consider the assets of WEA to be safer, and we think that we can profit from the more active management approach of the CEF's management team, WEA stands as the better opportunity any time it trades at a discount. Depending on sentiment, WEA has had periods of trading at a premium:

It's also a fund that has never had a wide discount. Although we don't believe there is significant alpha in high yields in general, WEA seems to be the better choice at its current valuation.

Conclusion

The market does not have a lot to offer. Stocks are at record valuations. Palantir is going for all kinds of world records. High-yield spreads are at historical lows. If everything is as bullish as it seems, WEA may be a nice addition to our portfolios.

data.title: Artisan Floating Rate Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Discussion

Our portfolio modestly underperformed the S&P UBS Leveraged Loan Index during the quarter. By rating, the portfolio benefited from security selection in B- and CCC-rated credits, while our overweight to CCC-rated debt detracted from returns. Across sectors, the largest positive contributors included security selection in retail and technology & electronics, while security selection within leisure detracted from returns.

Investing Environment

In a stark reversal of Q3, Treasury yields rose significantly across the curve as investors recalibrated interest rate expectations following the US elections, hawkish Federal Reserve commentary amid persistent inflation readings and a continued strong economic environment.

During the quarter, the 10-year Treasury yield rose 79bps and erased its entire decline from Q3 — ending the year close to its peak for 2024. In addition, the yield curve continued to steepen as the 2–10 spread rose 20bps with the yield curve ending the year at its steepest point since May 2022. All told, the 10-year Treasury yield ended the year up 95bps from its low in September, creating a volatile environment for duration-sensitive assets.

Against this backdrop, the loan market continued to generate positive returns in Q4, ending 2024 with full-year returns of nearly double digits. The S&P UBS Leveraged Loan Index returned 2.3% for the quarter and 9.1% for the year, aided by attractive coupon income and price gains. The loan index registered positive returns for each month of 2024 and provided a stable base of return in a year with high interest rate-driven volatility negatively impacting fixed rate investments. In addition, the index provided its 30th year of positive calendar year returns in its 33rd year of existence. The average price of the loan index rose over a full point during the year, with coupon spreads remaining relatively flat (down 22bps) on the back of continued positive technicals (low net issuance) and repricings.

As leveraged loans continue to generate consistent coupon income with low volatility, it’s worth putting into context their attractive relative and risk-adjusted returns across both recent and longer-term periods. On a trailing 1-, 3-, 5- and 10-year basis, the S&P UBS Leveraged Loan Index has outperformed both the ICE BofA US High Yield Index and the ICE BofA US Broad Market Index (a reference for the investment grade bond universe). In each of those periods, the loan index’s return per unit of volatility (standard deviation) was significantly higher than its fixed rate counterparts. In addition, the low correlation of the loan index to investment grade bonds (such as Treasurys, mortgage-backed securities and corporates) during this period provided a valuable complement to traditional asset allocations that may be more heavily dominated by fixed rate assets. It’s not often that you can find a diversifying asset with compelling absolute and risk-adjusted return potential, but investors have found these characteristics in loans over the past 10 years.

One of the most notable themes in credit markets in 2024 was the robustness of capital markets activity. On a gross supply basis, high yield issuance increased more than 60% year-over-year while leveraged loans recorded their highest annual issuance ever, significantly surpassing previous peaks in 2021 and 2017. Many issuers took advantage of more favorable market pricing and positive technicals to refinance and reprice their debt, extending maturities and achieving interest cost savings, thus reducing credit risk.

Nevertheless, as we have discussed in the past, net issuance—gross supply minus refinancings and repricings—remains low, driven by an M&A market that is still depressed relative to 2021. We believe that M&A will likely rebound in 2025, driven by a more pro-business administration, less restrictive Federal Trade Commission (FTC) approval process and deregulatory environment, which could create more net supply for our market. This dynamic may also result in increased interest from higher credit quality strategic buyers to acquire leveraged companies, introducing opportunities for investors to reap benefits as discounted debt of an acquisition target could reprice to par or above.

Excluding distressed exchanges, default rates in high yield and loans have been declining since early 2023 and are well below long-term averages. The most significant increase in headline defaults in 2024 came in the form of liability management exercises, or LMEs. While the frequency of mentions of “creditor-on-creditor violence” in the financial press seems to imply that LMEs are ubiquitous, in reality they continue to represent a very small part of the market. For example, according to JPMorgan data, 48 companies completed a distressed exchange in 2024; in total, there are estimated to be over 1,800 companies across leveraged credit markets. We continue to monitor this space and selectively participate across our platform. Often, the threat of an LME results in downward pressure on asset prices through secondary market selling. Our team has experience spanning the gamut from performing to distressed, enabling us to dig in deep on heavily discounted assets to potentially find value.

Portfolio Positioning

The portfolio remains invested primarily in single B-rated loans, with a small allocation to high yield bonds. Owing to our philosophy centered around high-conviction investing, the top 10 issuers represented approximately 30% of the portfolio at the end of the year. It’s worth noting that cash was relatively elevated at quarter-end following inflows near the end of December. We were comfortable holding a higher than average cash balance with an eye toward deploying in early January.

Credit discipline remains a critical part of our process. With a large amount of the loan market trading near par today, we believe that protection against permanent capital impairment is of the utmost importance. Our selectivity in 2024 is a testament to this fact; over 1,100 loan deals came to market in 2024, including repricings, refinancings and net new supply. We participated in only 54 of them, rejecting 95% of the new deals in the primary market. We believe this discipline is particularly important in an environment where many issuers have repriced deals significantly tighter, thereby reducing return potential to lenders. We witnessed many repricings in the market where we may have previously invested at wider spreads but declined to participate in the new deal, as we felt we were not properly compensated for the risk of the underlying issuer.

In Q4, our most significant addition was in a specialty foods manufacturer with a portfolio of protein and fitness bar brands. We purchased the S+500 first lien loans at new issue with a 94.5 original issue discount (OID) and creditor friendly documentation—a relative rarity in today’s loan market. We became comfortable with the business following extensive due diligence and calls with senior management. Given structured call protection above par, our portfolio would reap the benefits of price appreciation if the debt is refinanced prior to maturity. In the interim, we believe the debt offers compelling risk-adjusted return potential for an already quality business that may benefit from a trend toward higher protein diets amid the increasing proliferation of GLP-1s.

Perspective

The leveraged loan market performed well in 2024, with elevated all-in yields and modest price gains helping to produce returns near double digits. Loans outperformed most fixed income asset classes during the year aided by their lack of interest rate sensitivity as well as higher coupon income. While leveraged credit markets continue to trade at tighter spread levels, we believe they will likely be rangebound in the near term absent an exogenous shock. In addition, current yield levels for leveraged loans offer what we believe to be compelling risk-adjusted total return potential and a valuable complement to asset allocations.

As we look forward to 2025, there remain pockets of idiosyncratic value in the loan market which we seek to take advantage of in our portfolio. We continue to believe that an investment approach centered on business quality, fundamental issuer strength and high-conviction portfolio construction is of utmost importance in today’s market. As our investors know, we will never sacrifice credit discipline for the sake of chasing yields and returns.

Carefully consider the Fund’s investment objective, risks and charges and expenses. This and other important information is contained in the Fund's prospectus and summary prospectus, which can be obtained by calling 800.344.1770. Read carefully before investing.

Current and future portfolio holdings are subject to risk. The value of portfolio securities selected by the investment team may rise or fall in response to company, market, economic, political, regulatory or other news, at times greater than the market or benchmark index. A portfolio’s environmental, social and governance (“ESG”) considerations may limit the investment opportunities available and, as a result, the portfolio may forgo certain investment opportunities and underperform portfolios that do not consider ESG factors. Fixed income securities carry interest rate risk and credit risk for both the issuer and counterparty and investors may lose principal value. In general, when interest rates rise, fixed income values fall. High income securities (junk bonds) are speculative, experience greater price volatility and have a higher degree of credit and liquidity risk than bonds with a higher credit rating. The portfolio typically invests a significant portion of its assets in lower-rated high income securities (e.g., CCC). Loans carry risks including insolvency of the borrower, lending bank or other intermediary. Loans may be secured, unsecured, or not fully collateralized, trade infrequently, experience delayed settlement, and be subject to resale restrictions. Private placement and restricted securities may not be easily sold due to resale restrictions and are more difficult to value. Use of derivatives may create investment leverage and increase the likelihood of volatility and risk of loss in excess of the amount invested. International investments involve special risks, including currency fluctuation, lower liquidity, different accounting methods and economic and political systems, and higher transaction costs. These risks typically are greater in emerging and less developed markets, including frontier markets.

S&P UBS Leveraged Loan Index is an unmanaged market value-weighted index designed to mirror the investable universe of the US dollar-denominated leveraged loan market. Loan facilities must be rated “BB” or lower by S&P, Moody’s or Fitch; only fully funded term loan facilities are included; and issuers must be domiciled in developed countries. ICE BofA US Broad Market Index tracks the performance of US dollar-denominated investment grade debt publicly issued in the US domestic market, including US Treasury, quasi-government, corporate, securitized and collateralized securities. With the exception of local currency sovereign debt, qualifying securities must have an investment grade rating (based on an average of Moody’s, S&P and Fitch). ICE BofA US High Yield Index measures the performance of below investment grade US dollar-denominated corporate bonds publicly issued in the US market. The index(es) are unmanaged; include net reinvested dividends; do not reflect fees or expenses; and are not available for direct investment.

This summary represents the views of the portfolio managers as of 31 Dec 2024. Those views may change, and the Fund disclaims any obligation to advise investors of such changes. For the purpose of determining the Fund’s holdings, securities of the same issuer are aggregated to determine the weight in the Fund. Securities named in the Commentary, but not listed here are not held in the Fund as of the date of this report. Portfolio holdings are subject to change without notice and are not intended as recommendations of individual securities. All information in this report, unless otherwise indicated, includes all classes of shares (except performance and expense ratio information) and is as of the date shown in the upper right hand corner. Portfolio statistics include accrued interest unless otherwise stated and may vary from the official books and records of the Fund. This material does not constitute investment advice.

The S&P 500 and S&P UBS Leveraged Loan Indices are products of S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC (“S&P DJI”) and/or its affiliates and has been licensed for use. Copyright © 2025 S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC, a division of S&P Global, Inc. All rights reserved. Redistribution or reproduction in whole or in part are prohibited without written permission of S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC. S&P® is a registered trademark of S&P Global and Dow Jones® is a registered trademark of Dow Jones Trademark Holdings LLC (“Dow Jones”). None of S&P DJI, Dow Jones, their affiliates or third party licensors makes any representation or warranty, express or implied, as to the ability of any index to accurately represent the asset class or market sector that it purports to represent and none shall have any liability for any errors, omissions, or interruptions of any index or the data included therein.

Source ICE Data Indices, LLC is used with permission. ICE® is a registered trademark of ICE Data Indices, LLC or its affiliates and BofA® is a registered trademark of Bank of America Corporation licensed by Bank of America Corporation and its affiliates ("BofA"), and may not be used without BofA's prior written approval. The index data referenced herein is the property of ICE Data Indices, LLC, its affiliates (“ICE Data”) and/or its third party suppliers and, along with the ICE BofA trademarks, has been licensed for use by Artisan Partners Limited Partnership. ICE Data and its Third Party Suppliers accept no liability in connection with the use of such index data or marks. See www.artisanpartners.com/ice-data.html for a full copy of the Disclaimer.

Attribution is used to evaluate the investment management decisions which affected the portfolio’s performance when compared to a benchmark index. Contribution to return is calculated by Bloomberg by multiplying a security’s daily total return multiplied by the daily weight compounded over the referenced timeframe and does not take into account expenses of the portfolio. Attribution is not exact, but should be considered an approximation of the relative contribution of each of the factors considered.

This material is provided for informational purposes without regard to your particular investment needs and shall not be construed as investment or tax advice on which you may rely for your investment decisions. Investors should consult their financial and tax adviser before making investments in order to determine the appropriateness of any investment product discussed herein.

Credit Quality Ratings are determined by Artisan Partners based on ratings from S&P and/or Moody's, which typically range from AAA (highest) to D (lowest). For securities rated by both S&P and Moody's, the higher of the two ratings was used, and those not rated by either agency have been categorized as Unrated/Not Rated. Ratings are applicable to the underlying portfolio securities, but not the portfolio itself, and are subject to change. Non-Investment Grade refers to fixed income securities with lower credit quality. Par represents the level a security trades at when its yield equals its coupon. Spread is the difference in yield between two bonds of similar maturity but different credit quality. Average Price is the aggregate market value of the fixed income securities in the portfolio. Duration estimates the sensitivity of underlying fixed income securities to changes in interest rates—the longer the duration, the greater the sensitivity to changes in interest rates. Investment Grade indicates above-average credit quality and lower default risk and is defined as a rating of BBB or higher by Standard and Poor’s and Fitch rating services and Baa or higher by Moody’s ratings service. Leveraged Loans are extended to companies or individuals that already have considerable amounts of debt. Standard Deviation defines how widely returns varied from an average over a given period of time. Higher deviation represents higher volatility.

Artisan Partners Funds offered through Artisan Partners Distributors LLC (APDLLC), member FINRA. APDLLC is a wholly owned broker/dealer subsidiary of Artisan Partners Holdings LP. Artisan Partners Limited Partnership, an investment advisory firm and adviser to Artisan Partners Funds, is wholly owned by Artisan Partners Holdings LP.

Original Post

data.title: BNY Mellon Global Emerging Markets Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
“We are more comfortable highlighting the longer-term opportunities in emerging markets, based on relatively higher levels of income growth, rapid increases in product penetration and scope for industry consolidation.”

Market Review

The most decisive event during the final quarter of the year, in terms of its influence upon global markets, was the US presidential election. The election ushered in Donald Trump’s second administration and was the catalyst for a sharp rally in US risk assets that typically came at the expense of other regions. Strong emerging-market gains in the third quarter were partially erased by concerns over trade wars and the strong US dollar, which was fueled by expectations of ‘higher for longer’ US interest rates and the continued robust performance of the US economy. Domestic problems in China and South Korea further weighed on sentiment.

Performance Summary

BNY Mellon Global Emerging Markets Fund outperformed its benchmark index, the MSCI Emerging Markets Index (“the index”), during the fourth quarter of 2024.

Other share classes are subject to different fees and expenses and would have achieved different results. Performance for periods less than 1 year is not annualized. Go to BNY Mellon Investment Management - Global Home - English for the fund's most recent month-end returns. Returns assume the reinvestment of dividends and capital gains, if any.

The Net Expenses is the actual fund expense ratio applicable to investors. Not all classes of shares may be available to all investors or through all broker-dealer platforms.

The fund's investment adviser, BNY Mellon Investment Adviser, Inc., has contractually agreed, until March 1, 2025, to waive receipt of its fees and/or assume the direct expenses of the fund so that the direct expenses of none of the fund's share classes (excluding Rule 12b-1 fees, shareholder services fees, taxes, interest expense, brokerage commissions, commitment fees on borrowings and extraordinary expenses) exceed 1.00%. On or after March 1, 2025, BNY Mellon Investment Adviser, Inc. may terminate this expense limitation agreement at any time.

The holdings listed should not be considered recommendations to buy or sell a security. Large concentrations can increase share price volatility.

The rally witnessed in Chinese and Hong Kong equity markets at the end of the previous quarter proved short-lived and the gains made were partially reversed. Widely anticipated stimulus measures from the Chinese government disappointed investors as they appeared piecemeal, lacked the significance to restimulate the economy, and concentrated on restructuring struggling sectors such as property or local government finances. Nevertheless, investors continued to anticipate additional stimulus initiatives from the Chinese government in 2025, perhaps to combat any trade-related weakness induced by potential changes to US trade policy. Onshore Chinese bond yields continued to fall and the yield on China’s 30-year bond fell below that of Japan.

In South Korea, the president surprised both domestic parliamentarians and world leaders with an order to impose martial law, citing opposition party collaboration with North Korea. This was quickly reversed as lawmakers took steps to remove presidential powers from the sitting president, with an impeachment vote late in the quarter. The effect on the equity market was limited, though pressure manifested in the continued weakness of the South Korean won. The Indian market, an investor darling of recent years, slipped as evidence of a cyclical downturn prompted investors to reduce exposure. In Brazil, the country’s budget deficit and fiscal position caused the stock market and Brazilian real to weaken significantly.

Conversely, renewed excitement around artificial intelligence (AI) meant that the technology-heavy Taiwanese market performed well.

Against this backdrop, the fund outperformed its benchmark. Strength in the consumer discretionary sector was led by the fund’s travel company stocks and a zero weighting in Alibaba. Within the information technology sector, the fund benefited from its Taiwanese holdings and the zero weighting in Samsung Electronics. In a weak commodity price environment, the zero weightings in energy and materials contributed as well.

Weakness within the consumer staples sector was mainly attributable to the holding in Unicharm (OTCPK:UNICY)(OTCPK:UNCHF). The holdings in Bank Mandiri (OTCPK:PPERF)(OTCPK:PPERY) and Chailease were the main detractors in the financials sector.

Top Contributors

There was a positive contribution from the fund’s Taiwanese holdings, headed by chip designer MediaTek, as its latest quarterly statutory earnings per share (EPS) came in materially higher than consensus estimates. This led market watchers to upgrade full-year revenue expectations. Testing equipment manufacturer Chroma ATE outperformed, as it reported expectations-beating second-quarter revenues and statutory earnings per share.

Both stocks and power supply manufacturer Delta Electronics (OTCPK:DLEGF) also did well amid renewed excitement around AI.

The fund benefited from its zero weighting in e-commerce giant Alibaba, which declined over the quarter on the lack of firm measures by the Chinese government to stimulate the domestic economy. A further positive came from not holding Samsung Electronics, which continued to struggle, with the company warning that its third-quarter profits would fall short of market expectations.

Chinese travel service provider Trip.com reported expectations-beating revenues, which were strongly up from the previous year and the previous quarter.

Shares in Indian online travel company MakeMyTrip (MMYT) hit a record high, driven by investor enthusiasm for its business model and growth prospects.

Top Detractors

The consumer staples sector was the main area of weakness during the fourth quarter. The holding in disposable hygiene product manufacturer Unicharm detracted, as it reported quarterly revenues and statutory earnings per share that fell short of market expectations amid an intensifying competitive environment in core markets. India’s Avenue Supermarkets was weak. The company reported a rise in revenue and profits in its latest quarterly update, but its shares fell sharply on disappointing like-for-like growth and margins, and on concerns about competition from online grocery platforms. Pharmacy chain Raia Drogasil (OTCPK:RADLY) fell amid a weak Brazilian market, as investors worried about the government’s fiscal policies.

Following strong performance in 2024, shares in e-commerce platform MercadoLibre retreated on concerns around slowing growth at its credit business.

Shares in new holding Anta Sports (OTCPK:ANPDY)(OTCPK:ANPDF) declined after the company reported that sales of its high-margin Fila branded products declined in the third quarter as cautious Chinese consumers downgraded to cheaper domestic brands. However, the company is still expected to achieve strong revenue growth in the second half of the year.

The zero weighting in China’s Xiaomi (OTCPK:XIACF)(OTCPK:XIACY) detracted, as its share price climbed, in part owing to the company’s successful entry into the electric-vehicle market.

An interest rate-sensitive holding, Bank Mandiri, underperformed in the ‘higher-for-longer’ rate environment.

Market Outlook

Over the short term, we believe asset prices will probably continue to be influenced by the inflationary forces in the US, along with the US Federal Reserve’s response. Other variables will probably influence the trajectory of emerging-market equities in the coming months, including the conflict in Ukraine, commodity prices, the US dollar’s strength, the evolution of the Chinese recovery, and global macro-financial conditions. Emerging-market equities currently trade at an unusually high discount to developed markets, which provides a conducive backdrop if these shorter-term variables prove favorable for emerging markets.

We are more comfortable highlighting the longer-term opportunities in emerging markets, based on relatively higher levels of income growth, rapid increases in product penetration and scope for industry consolidation. We believe there is a unique opportunity for emerging-market companies that are well exposed to reliable secular growth trends and that can exploit this opportunity more than their peers, with their differentiated customer offering and execution. Accordingly, we believe that emerging-market investors who can identify the right growth themes and companies should be rewarded over the long term.

Risks

Equities are subject to market, market sector, market liquidity, issuer, and investment style risks, to varying degrees. Investing in foreign denominated and/or domiciled securities involves special risks, including changes in currency exchange rates, political, economic, and social instability, limited company information, differing auditing and legal standards, and less market liquidity. These risks generally are greater with emerging market countries.

Index Definitions

The MSCI Emerging Markets Index is a free float-adjusted, market capitalization-weighted index that is designed to measure the equity performance in global emerging markets. The index consists of 22 MSCI emerging-market national indices. An investor cannot invest directly in any index.

Definitions
